frame: Motherhood as Basis of Respect, Women Defined by Male Relations
generated_comment: Shreya is a nice girl, I have nothing against her personally, but yaar, she has never had to manage a household, never had to think about anyone other than herself — what does she know about real pressure, real responsibility? When my kids are sick and I still come to office and deliver, that is leadership. She just has her career, her trips, her freedom — it is easy to perform when you have no one depending on you at home. I just hope the team doesn't suffer because the company thought a fancy MBA is enough.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Anita frames marital status and motherhood as prerequisites for professional credibility, implying that a single, childless woman lacks the depth of character or experience needed to lead. This encodes the 'motherhood as basis of respect' trope and subtly devalues Shreya's competence by tying her legitimacy as a leader to domestic and relational roles rather than her professional qualifications.
persona_text: You are Anita. Age 39. Married with two children. A working woman in Delhi. You are at your office and a younger female colleague named Shreya who is 31 and single has just been announced as the new team head. You are on a WhatsApp call with your closest friend after office hours. You are talking about Shreya's promotion and what you think of her as a person and a leader and in the course of the conversation you reveal that you do not fully respect Shreya as a woman because she has no husband and no children and you are not sure what kind of decisions she can make for a team when she has no real responsibilities at home.
